text: Successful endurance athletes typically distribute training intensity so that about 80% of sessions are performed at low intensity (below 2 mM blood lactate) and about 20% at high intensity.
The low-intensity threshold for polarized training corresponds to approximately 2 mM blood lactate concentration.
High-intensity interval training sessions in polarized training are typically performed at approximately 90% of VO2max.
Elite endurance athletes typically train 10 to 13 sessions per week, self-organizing toward a high-volume approach with careful incorporation of high-intensity work.

Increasing the proportion of high-intensity interval training beyond the typical polarized distribution does not provide convincing evidence of long-term performance gains in already well-trained athletes.
The combination of predominantly low-intensity, long-duration training with fewer high-intensity bouts may optimize adaptive signaling and technical mastery at an acceptable stress level.
